perturbation.original_text: ETON shall enter into a commercial supply agreement with a contract manufacturing organization and Aucta shall enter into a commercial supply agreement with an active pharmaceutical ingredient supplier within ninety (90) days from the Execution Date unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: ETON shall enter into a commercial supply agreement with a contract manufacturing organization and Aucta shall enter into a commercial supply agreement with an active pharmaceutical ingredient supplier within one (1) day from the Execution Date unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This change drastically reduces the timeframe for establishing commercial supply agreements to one day. This is likely an unreasonably short timeframe that is not operationally feasible to execute, which could be seen as an unconscionable practice, and could thus run contrary to general principles of contract law. There are no federal laws about royalty payment dates, so we rely on state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law says that a contract is unconscionable if "at the time it is made [it] is unconscionable". Setting an expectation of a full commerical supply agreement in 1 day, which includes various legal and financial reviews, is unlikely and would be considered unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 810 ILCS 5/2-302 (810 ILCS 5/2-302) (from Ch. 26, par. 2-302) Sec. 2-302. Unconscionable contract or clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Source: Laws 1961, p. 2101.) (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. perturbation.justification: The modified text drastically reduces the timeframe for establishing commercial supply agreements from 90 days to 1 day. This creates a high likelihood of being deemed unconscionable, because setting an expectation of a full commercial supply agreement in 1 day, which includes various legal and financial reviews, is unlikely and would be considered unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, both quoting Illinois Compiled Statutes 810 ILCS 5/2-302, state that "If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract". This demonstrates a direct contradiction because the modified text's timeframe is likely unconscionable under Illinois law, making it unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: If either Party shall learn of (a) any claim or assertion that the manufacture, use or marketing of the Product under this Agreement, or any other action taken by either party in performance of its obligations hereunder infringes, misappropriates or otherwise violates the intellectual property rights of any Third Party, or (b) the actual or threatened infringement, misappropriation or other violation by any Third Party of the intellectual property rights of any party that are the subject of this Agreement ("Intellectual Rights Suits"), then the Party becoming so informed shall as soon as reasonably practicable, but in all events within three (3) Business Days thereafter (the "Infringement Notification Date"), notify the other Party of such claim or assertion, or actual or threatened infringement, misappropriation or other violation.
perturbation.changed_text: If either Party shall learn of (a) any claim or assertion that the manufacture, use or marketing of the Product under this Agreement, or any other action taken by either party in performance of its obligations hereunder infringes, misappropriates or otherwise violates the intellectual property rights of any Third Party, or (b) the actual or threatened infringement, misappropriation or other violation by any Third Party of the intellectual property rights of any party that are the subject of this Agreement ("Intellectual Rights Suits"), then the Party becoming so informed shall notify the other Party of such claim or assertion, or actual or threatened infringement, misappropriation or other violation when convenient.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of any specific deadline and replacing it with vague language such as 'when convenient' for notifying the other party about intellectual property infringement claims creates uncertainty. This can prejudice the affected party's ability to take timely action to protect its rights. The original provides for a clear requirement to notify in '3 business days' and replacing this with when convenient introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement
perturbation.law_explanation: This provides a cause of action for trademark infringment. Delaying notice of potential IP infringements directly affects the ability to act to stem infringement and is related to remedies. Therefore this affects material rights and can be interpreted as contradictory.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'within three (3) Business Days' to 'when convenient' directly contradicts the obligation to provide timely notification regarding intellectual property infringement claims. This affects the ability to take action to stem infringement. 15 USC 1114 outlines remedies for infringement, making the ability to act quickly materially important. The original text provides a clear and enforceable deadline, while the modified text introduces ambiguity that could prejudice the affected party's ability to protect its rights. The change weakens a core obligation and is contradictory. Both snippets support the idea of providing remedies to stem infringment, and this is clearly damaged by the change.
